name: Laramie
short_description: BLM public lands near Laramie offer accessible rockhounding for quartz, feldspar, and mica in the high Laramie Range. Free personal use collecting with a 25 lb daily limit on gentle terrain.
description:
The Laramie area sits within the Laramie Range, a basement rock province dominated by Archean-age granite and metamorphic rocks. BLM lands surrounding this high-elevation basin provide excellent opportunities for recreational rockhounders to collect quartz crystals, feldspar, and mica without permits or fees. The terrain is relatively gentle with good road access, making it suitable for both beginners and families.

This region's geology reflects ancient Precambrian continental crust uplifted during Laramide orogeny. Weathering and erosion of the granitic bedrock have exposed excellent collecting zones along washes, ridgelines, and exposed outcrops. Quartz occurs in veins and as large crystals within the granite matrix, while feldspar and mica are abundant in the host rock itself.

The high elevation (7,652 feet) and semi-arid climate create a dry, accessible landscape. Rockhounders typically find specimens in loose material along drainages and on hillsides with minimal digging required. The gentle slope around this GPS location makes walking and collecting straightforward for most ages and abilities.

Personal use collection is permitted on BLM lands under FLPMA regulations. This is an excellent day-trip destination from Laramie city proper, with multiple access points along the surrounding public lands. The combination of free access, good specimens, and manageable terrain makes this a popular local rockhounding area.
history: The Laramie Range is composed of Archean-age granite and metamorphic basement rocks uplifted during the Laramide orogeny (70-55 million years ago). Millions of years of erosion and weathering have exposed the granitic intrusions and created abundant surface specimens of quartz, feldspar, and mica that weathering continues to liberate from the bedrock.
tips: Search along dry washes and drainage areas where water action has concentrated loose crystals and mineral fragments. Look for quartz veins cutting through exposed granite outcrops on hillsides and ridge faces. Bring a small hammer and chisel to carefully extract larger specimens from vugs in the granite; the host rock is relatively soft and specimens often come free with gentle work. Focus your efforts in areas with visible weathered granite and avoid sealed private property boundaries.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7,652 feet in the Laramie Range with gentle slopes and semi-arid grass and sagebrush vegetation. Loose rock and gravelly footing is common. Walking distance from parking varies from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on access point chosen. Terrain is uneven but navigable by most fit rockhounders; sturdy hiking boots recommended.
best_season: June - September. The high elevation means snow and ice close many access roads October through May. Summer and early fall offer dry conditions, good road access, and comfortable collecting temperatures.
written_directions: From Laramie, take WY-130 (Snowy Range Road) south and east toward the Snowy Range ski area. Look for unmarked BLM access points along the road approximately 8-15 miles from town. Pullouts and informal parking areas exist at several locations along WY-130 and adjacent Forest Service roads. Consult a BLM recreation map or contact the Laramie Field Office for specific coordinates of developed access areas. Roads are typically passable for passenger vehicles in summer; high-clearance vehicles recommended for rougher branch roads.
road_conditions: WY-130 is paved. BLM branch roads vary from graded dirt to rough track; generally passable for passenger vehicles in dry season, high-clearance preferred.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along WY-130 and BLM roads. No developed parking lot. Space is typically available but can be limited on weekends. Arrive early to secure a spot near productive collecting areas.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect all private property boundaries and posted closure areas. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect private property boundaries
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Laramie, approximately 8-15 miles west. Full services including gas, food, water, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Gentle slopes and relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved roads to parking areas and short walking distances to collecting sites. No wheelchair access on rough BLM roads. Sturdy hiking boots and moderate fitness recommended.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you find rockhounding near Laramie, Wyoming?
faq.answer: The Laramie area is known for quartz crystals, feldspar, and mica collected from weathered granite outcrops on BLM lands. The high-elevation Laramie Range contains abundant Archean granite with excellent quartz specimens in vugs and veins. You will also find colorful feldspar and sheets of mica throughout the region.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound on BLM land near Laramie?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on BLM lands near Laramie. FLPMA regulations allow recreational collecting without a permit, though you must follow the 25 lb per day limit and avoid motorized excavation. Always check with the Laramie BLM Field Office if you have questions about specific access areas.

faq.question: Is rockhounding near Laramie suitable for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, the Laramie area is beginner and family-friendly. The gentle terrain, accessible parking, and short walking distances make it ideal for children ages 6 and up. Many specimens weather free from the host rock with minimal digging, so basic tools and light effort yield good results for inexperienced rockhounders.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding in the Laramie area?
faq.answer: The best season is June through September. The high elevation (7,652 feet) means snow and ice block access October through May. Summer months offer dry conditions, open roads, and comfortable collecting temperatures ideal for outdoor rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need to find quartz in Laramie?
faq.answer: A small hammer and chisel or pry bar work well for extracting specimens from granite outcrops and weathered rock. Wear sturdy hiking boots for uneven terrain and bring a day pack to carry your 25 lb daily limit. A magnifying glass helps identify feldspar and mica in the field. Sunscreen and water are essential at the high elevation.

faq.question: How close is the Laramie rockhounding area to town and what are the nearest services?
faq.answer: BLM collecting areas near Laramie are 8-15 miles from the city via WY-130 (Snowy Range Road), making this an easy day trip. Laramie provides full services including gas stations, restaurants, grocery stores, and lodging. Most access points along WY-130 have informal pullouts; arrive early on weekends to secure parking near productive sites.

meta_description: Rockhound for quartz, feldspar, and mica on free BLM lands near Laramie, Wyoming. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Beginner-friendly high-elevation collecting.
